sentences.text: No, until March '38, life was normal.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because actually, it was Mussolini who saved the Jews in Austria once.
In 1933, the Germans wanted to invade them.
And he put troops on the border-- German Italian border, Austrian border.
Seven divisions.
He said, either you get out or stop it.
At that time, Hitler wasn't strong enough, yet, so he stopped it.
They couldn't do anything, because they didn't-- they were not sure whether some spy had been listening and report it.
And in this connection, something interesting for the record.
After-- that was around '36 or so-- a German Jewish refugee who came to Vienna, very educated man, blonde, blue-eyed, tall, said he never would have been taken for a Jew.
So in Berlin, he went to one of these meetings.
And he told me, he said the most frightening thing to me was while I was in the meeting, I went ahead with it at first like the others.
Mass hypnosis.
Said it took me five minutes until after I left the meeting to wake up.
While I was there, I was hypnotized by him.
Yeah.
Leave Europe.
Leave Europe all together.
Yes, we wanted to leave but you couldn't because no other country wanted to let you in, and let you work there.
Because all of them were in a very bad financial shape, and there were a lot of unemployment-- for years before.
Since 1917, the whole thing started. 1917 was the-- the beginning of the European catastrophe.
Then, that was-- that was, I think, just in the beginning when they came to Vienna, one of my mother's sisters had been in touch with some distant relatives in the United States, very vaguely.
So I asked for the address.
So she gave it to me.
And she said don't expect too much.
I wrote.
Anyhow, they sent me an affidavit.
But then I had to wait for the quota number.
And the quota for Austria was 190-something per year.
And for Germany, it was restricted to about 700 or 800 per year, which was absolutely ridiculous.
And actually it was Roosevelt.
We were not able.
Shanghai was about the only place.
Or-- and even Israel, the British didn't want anybody to get in.
You know the story of that.
I don't have to repeat that.
Pardon?
Nothing.
One of the Nazis passed by and for some excuse they took me.
They didn't need an excuse.
He came in.
I had [INAUDIBLE].
And it-- actually, it was not a weapon because the end was sealed.
But I-- he didn't even realize fully I should come with them to deliver it to the party there.
So I knew that there were two others, I knew that was it.
So he came there, and he wanted to take me into the room where the particular members were.
But they didn't let him.
The other already go there.
So I knew then what it was.
And there I want to mention specifically an incident that always impressed me very unfavorably and deeply.
It was mostly high school, upper high school, college students, that-- who were the main contingent in that particular case of the Nazi group.
They pushed us Jews, pushed into the cellar.
Hardly any space.
And they fight with each other.
They took a father and a son, and they wanted them to hit each other.
But then one of my formal fellow schoolmates who had gone through, through the fourth, and who knew me well, he took me out.
So I don't know what happened further there.
But then from then on, I was registered and so-- but then, I knew that was the way to Dachau.
And--
I was not arrested.
The Gestapo directly said, protective custody.
Not arrested-- protective custody.
Whom they tried to protect, I don't know.
They didn't say anything.
But when you want to-- we were there-- the only way was to get some way through leave Europe.
So I was after father-- I was after Father, I had to get out.
So when I was released in February 21, so I told Father-- he went to the German official travel agency.
You see, at that time the Germans took big advantages, especially from the party.
They had fake travel agencies, collected money from the Jews for ships that never went.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So Father went to the official German travel agency and they had sell one ticket for the trip to leave.
So he got it for me.
What?
The ticket I got probably around the era of I left the camp the 21st of February, came to Vienna about to 23rd or so, and from then on, I worked in a church.
Would have been probably the end of February, beginning of March.
Not even protective custody, you didn't know from one moment what would happen.
Every one of those storm troopers, you were at their-- their will-- ill-will, not good will.
They tried to put fear into you.
And what we worried was in the middle of the winter, we wore nothing protective.
The shirts had no collar, nothing.
The shoes were old army boots that were so stiffened leather, you could hardly walk in them.
No shoe laces.
No it wasn't.
It was a camp.
What would you call it?
One of those trains for 40 men and 40 horses.
You know what I mean by that?
Freight.
And not only that, but and every station that the train took because it was a freight train, had to let all the other trains ahead, just to frighten as they started rattling the doors and so forth.
Huh?
It's so hard to tell.
But at least a day or two.
It was Vienna to Munich, and then from there to Dachau.
And being a freight train, it had to stop with all the other regular trains.
Nothing.
We were just locked up.
And it a way it was good that we were locked up, otherwise I might have done more mischief.
I closed my eyes and tried to sleep through the whole thing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because what they would have done and said would have only been to frighten the others.
And then I decided I have to look out for myself now.
I knew Dachau.
One of the camps, anyhow.
Nothing, just the best or the worst.
They're still only rumors.
It's hard to pin that down, because they were not based on facts.
Because those people who had been there didn't talk about it, because if they talked about it and the Gestapo knew it, and they went right back.
You were supposed to not to say a word, to anybody.
Which way?
Anything could happen.
Because the first three, four weeks about 300 or 400 of the imprisoned refugees died.
And the joker of it was they were cremated, but then their relatives, the family were notified that they had to pay for the cremation and they would get the ashes.
No, the first, it was late in the evening.
So they put us into a room, we slept on straw all together.
And there was one temporarily in charge-- a former communist.
Not a Jew.
So we didn't know where he was, so we kept quiet.
You didn't talk to anybody about anything.
You could tell general conversation, but not political.
You couldn't trust anybody.
Were the people in your particular transport, were they all Jewish?
Or a mix?
It was all Jewish.
See, there in the camp, we had two separate blocks, buildings.
There were two big buildings to each block.
And we were two or three blocks only for Jews.
And then later on, that this may interest you-- one day we noticed a cross on the other side of the main street.
And there was gates put up, and there were young Jewish children.
And they were up to the age of 14.
I don't know from where they came.
And they were kept there until these two, three weeks.
Then what happened to them, we don't know.
One day they were not there anymore.
In the camp?
No, actually that time is was still mainly for Germans and Austrians.
I don't know about the other camps.
You see, that was different, too.
But Dachau was specifically for Germans and Austrians.

And it was already getting ready for the Sudeten Germans, because they had all registered when they marched into Sudeten Bohemia, they arrested about 30,000 Sudeten Germans, not Jews-- because they were not Nazis.
Fear.
You didn't-- you thought-- you tried to think as little as possible.
Not-- It varied, too.
It was in winter where it was miserable, very often cold and rainy and snowy.
And we had to walk, constantly walk.
And there was so funny-- [INAUDIBLE] contradictions.
First, they said we should sing.
So we're German singing songs.
And after they decided, no, Jews shouldn't sing German songs.
We had to sing something else.
Then they stop the singing.
They didn't know themselves.
They improvised from one day to the other.
And outside of the camp, you couldn't escape because there was a big vacation for SS troops.
Luckily, they ate the same food as we, so it wasn't too bad.
Very little.
And we had no meat.
The only meat we had was [INAUDIBLE].
Otherwise, all vegetable.
They had facilities, but we couldn't use it properly.
Once a week or so, or two weeks, they took you and put you into the showers.
Otherwise, they had like a toilet room and bath, and we shower with faucets.
You could wash yourself-- that was all you could do.
You could even shave.
You was supposed to shave.
You slept.
When we slept, most of the time until later on, it was just nothing but on the floor.
And what they used there was old covers from horses-- dirty and filthy and what-- it was good enough for us.
Another prisoner.
There were two prisoners, one for each of these rooms.
And they were in charge, but they themselves had to behave, too, because they were prisoners themselves.
But because being a favorite, they didn't have to go through all the discipline that the others had to go through.
As a rule, they were fairly decent.
They didn't, so far as I know-- except one fellow who was half crazy, he famous or infamously famous.
But as a rule, they treated us pretty decently.
They let us know that we were lower than they, but still, they didn't take it out on us.
He was awful.
He mistreated everybody as much as he could.
No, that was in another room.
See, there were-- four-- in a block, there were three or four separate room.
Actually it was two rooms.
The one where you ate, and the other one where you slept.
Half the time we slept on straw on the floor.
What?
I don't remember any names from there.
About 4 o'clock in the morning.
Had to wash up a little bit, get ready.
Then stand for appel-- it was a roll call.
We had to be there on the main square, regardless of whether it's 6 o'clock in the morning, the roll call.
Then after the roll call, we went back for breakfast.
And-- and everywhere along the wall of the camp, were towers where there was always an SS man with machine gun.
And he was at freedom to shoot.
Nobody would control him.
We saw once a shot man-- they intentionally led us by the corpse.
They said he tried to escape, which was ridiculous.
How could he escape?
But they sometimes did to create a means of scaring the other people.
The first one to walk towards a fence, and then they shot him because-- escape.
You couldn't escape there.
It was all surrounded by the SS troops.
No, it was on top of it, yes.
But it was mainly these barbed wires.
And strong wiring.
And the also a trench with water.
Nothing, just empty field.
It was wide-- in the-- it was farm country-- wide open fields.
We didn't even see the SS vacations because they were on the other side.
Even though when you went out, then you saw them.
But when you were in the camp, you didn't see them.
No.
Walking, then from the beginning, singing.
Then walking, walking until then later on they got tired of that, too.
So we were in our rooms, not knowing what to do with each other.
We tried to maintain a certain atmosphere that permitted us to do a little thinking, but you didn't talk because you didn't know whether the other fellow wouldn't report you.
And among the Jewish, spies for the Gestapo, too.
They had penalties.
Like for one thing, they inspected our eating facilities.
So there was maybe a trifle, not fully a screen-- of 40 times up and down.
40 down into the knees, then you had to lift and fast, 40 times.
And there were people who supposedly they never took anybody over 65.
But there are many people in their 80s from Germany there.
And many people died.
There was one, a young man, a [NON-ENGLISH], former [NON-ENGLISH]-- the [NON-ENGLISH] was a Jewish sports organization.
I never saw him any-- he was lying there in his bunk.
No food, nothing, he gradually until he died.
But they had a little hospital.
But that was too good for Jews.
That was for the other prisoners.
The other prisoners had to work.
Very stiff.
Not at the time.
That came later on.
Not in my time, luckily.
For example, among the prisoners, two of the sons of the crown prince, Ferdinand of Austria-- and they were treated just like any other prisoner.
Not with the Jews, but they were there, and we saw them every day.
And they always took up on themselves automatically the heaviest loads to be an example, because they hated the Nazis.
I don't know what they did in the field, but they had very heavy carts, and they had to be moved by people.
And if that slipped back and you got under the wheel, forget it.
One of those two sons died that way.
Yeah.
Yeah, but remember it was long platform cars.
Talking, doing nothing, thinking, dreaming.
Some probably had it, but usually those members of the Zionist organization, but we didn't.
Because we didn't trust each other.
Yeah, enemy, I don't know enemy.
But he was the one-- you see at that time, they started taking some of the Jews under 30.
And we were standing on the appel square, so the fella ahead of me-- I don't know was he afraid that he might be called.
He told the SS man, take this fellow.
He's under 30.
I was past 30.
He tried to deny it, so the SS man got sick and tired and walked away.
The Jews were their own worst enemies very often.
I hate to say that.
Like this one fellow.
You didn't know with whom to talk and what to talk.
Because you never knew sometimes-- they took him in and made him talk.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was always full of possibilities even if they didn't take place.
To a greater extent, fear.
They couldn't anymore trust each other.
Because I never knew whether one of them wouldn't be forced to talk, or invent things, or whether he volunteered.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] See, fear makes-- funny things to people.
You look out for your only-- own skin, or you don't survive.
Anything could be depends on how-- even if you didn't say to them, they might say you said it.
And your word didn't mean anything.
So you tried to stay out of the limelight.
They tried it, bit it didn't really work.
Because every one of us hoped to be released to go.
It wasn't something like more of an organization, because tomorrow I might not be there, or the other fellow might not be there.
Within the block itself, there was more closeness, because you didn't know the other block.
You didn't go and talk to them.
The non-Jewish prisoners could, but we couldn't.
And we smoked, we had to smoke outside in the corridor.
Because they're all wooden buildings, which was logical.
We couldn't smoke in there.
We could buy them.
They were very happy to sell us anything, but only what they wanted to.
We could buy white bread, but we couldn't buy dark bread.
The dark bread was good.
The white bread naturally was more nourishing, so we could buy it and it cost more.
They had like a canteen.
You know what that is?
And in the country, that I'll show you in [INAUDIBLE], once the International Red Cross sent some inspection there.
They only looked at the canteen, they never looked at the camp-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as if it wouldn't be there.
Not-- internationally.
We saw them from the distance.
We couldn't come to them.
Four or five of them, in the red badge-- a higher officer of the SS, he only show them what they wanted them to see.
And everything was beautiful.
Sometimes the parents or family could send certain amounts at certain terms of the month.
Yeah, receive it, but to send it, you had to write it on a piece of paper that probably was all checked anyhow.
And then it was mailed to the family.
What possession did they have?
Handkerchief?
The money?
Yes.
But you spent it because you could have it also only up to a certain amount.
No.
The money that I had before that was taken when I was arrested.
Then afterwards, Father-- every four or five weeks, he sent me some.
That helped me to survive.
That was officially-- again, one every four or five weeks we could write, but that was an open letter, and they checked out what you wrote.
Yeah, get me out.
Get me out.
And hoping that they were well.
I tried one time in this one block where I tried to organize a discussion.
But it didn't work out, because everybody was concerned with himself.
And I, myself, too.
Because as I say, the worst aspect of it was, you didn't know from one moment to the other what would happen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You were fully at the discretion of the SS.
Well, for example they started exercising us in the dirt.
And afterwards, we had to wash it first.
In the mud, outside.
Things like that.
Where once, he was there-- a young SS man, we knew later that he was an SS man, in a uniform of one of the prisoners.
Maybe they just planted him.
Anyhow the freedom-- he tyrannized to no end to collect all his cigarette slips from the others, from the place there all over.
But we couldn't stand or something.
We had to go, how should I say?
We bent down.
And it could take an hour or two.
Just to humiliate everybody.
Pardon?
Not only humiliate, but it was a physical strain, too.
We saw only the one.
Which they showed to us.
But we knew that people always-- we didn't know did they disappear or did they-- were they released?
No.
In that moment, they took you out in-- to-- to the crematory and cremated you and send the bill to the family.
We never saw that.
Because you tried to see as little as possible.
They were, but we didn't know about them.
And maybe they were not then, yet.
It was an early time.
That we didn't see.
Because if that would have been outside of the camp.
It wouldn't have been in the camp, itself.
You see, our camp was only a part of the overall camp.
And we didn't see anything outside of the wired fences.
We didn't know.
Except the rumors.
We knew that there were crematories.
SS, he could stop you, but you had to stand at least five feet away.
Otherwise he could shoot you.
That you aggressed him.
No.
They could call you all kinds of names, but you couldn't answer.
